chapters.title: Careers do not have to be straight lines
chapters.text:
Close your eyes and picture yourself at sixty five or seventy. Are you sitting on a beach with a drink in your hand, or does the picture feel impossible? If the second option sounds more familiar, you are not alone. Most people who work today do not expect to retire in the same way their parents and grandparents did.

So much for the beach. The good news is that there is a path forward, and it starts with a simple idea. The old corporate ladder is gone. We work in a world that experts call VUCA, which is short for volatile, uncertain, complex, and ambiguous. The career path of today is not a straight line. It zigzags, and the only way to win is to make peace with the curves.

Try being a learn-it-all instead of a know-it-all. Set a goal to pick up a new skill every few months.

Even with the noise, the modern world gives you a chance that did not exist before. You can do work that you actually love, not the work that other people tell you to do. To do that, you need five career skills, and they are all in this summary. Let us get started.

chapters.title: From average to remarkable
chapters.text:
The first building block is your superstrengths. The idea is as old as stories about heroes, but when was the last time you actually sat down and thought about yours? Not just the rough idea, but a real list of what you are good at and what you naturally bring to the table. Pull out a pen and a notebook and run through the four steps below to get a clear picture of who you are.

Step one: First bursts. Set a timer for one minute and write down twenty things you are good at. It can be harder than it sounds, so include some strengths that are not part of your job. Then write three weaknesses, and try to turn each one into a strength on the same page. For example, if public speaking makes you freeze, you can flip that into a real strength for listening to others.

Step two: Natural talents. Talents are easy to miss in yourself, so ask friends or colleagues to write down three of your strengths with a short note on why they picked each one. Compare their answers to the list you made in step one, and pull out the top six natural talents that show up in both.

Step three: Learned strengths. Think about what you have picked up over the years. Walk through your CV and your LinkedIn profile and list the tasks you have done and the skills you have picked up along the way.

Show that you take learning seriously by writing down the courses you have taken and the skills you have built.

Step four: Find your superstrengths. A strength is something you are good at, and a superstrength is something you are brilliant at. Pick the six strengths you feel most sure about and rate each of them on a scale of one to ten across four areas. Success is how much it pays off in your work life. Frequency is how often you get to use it. Openness is how visible it is to the people around you. Happiness is how much it lights you up inside.

Now look at the strength with the highest happiness score. Try to push the other scores for that strength higher. That is how a strength becomes a superstrength.

chapters.title: The compass for your work life
chapters.text:
A career without a compass is just a series of jobs, and the compass is your values. Values are what make you you. They guide the small decisions, and they keep you honest with yourself. To get a clear picture of your own values, try the five exercises below.

The first exercise is reflection. Take a piece of paper and draw your career as a line, with the highs and the lows. Make notes about how you felt at each point, and pull out three must-haves and three must-nots for the next chapter. For example, a must-have could be a friendly team, and a must-not could be a culture of overtime.

The second exercise is spotting. Ask yourself what really matters to you, and keep asking until the answers start to repeat. Pay attention to the words that come up more than once.

The third exercise is scanning. Look over this list and mark the words that pull at you. Justice, privacy, fun, learning, freedom, friendship, courage, challenge, wealth, safety, growth, control, diversity, influence, vision, health, belonging, self-respect, discipline, newness, loyalty, logic, independence, and partnership.

The fourth exercise is prioritizing. From the lists you have built, pick the ten values that matter most. Put them in a table with two columns. Take the first value, say growth, and compare it to the second value, say freedom. If growth feels more important to you, put a tick next to it. Then move down the table and compare growth to every other value. Do the same for each value on the list. The values with the most ticks are the ones that matter most to you.

The fifth exercise is defining. Take the top four values from the last step and write a one-sentence definition for each one. This is where you really feel the weight of what each value means to you.

Knowing your values is not enough. You have to use them. One way to start is to bring them into your daily work. If innovation is one of your top values, ask your manager if you can take on a task that involves testing a new idea.

Your most powerful career tool is the person you already are. Find that person and let them lead.

chapters.title: Become the main character of your career
chapters.text:
Imagine walking into a room and feeling the attention land on you without trying. There is no magic in it, and the answer is confidence. Confidence is not something you either have or do not, and it is not just for outgoing people. Anyone can grow it, and the benefits of confidence are bigger than skills or knowledge alone.

The first thing to do is to face the confidence gremlins. A gremlin is that little voice in your head that says you are not smart enough or that you are too young to apply for a job. Gremlins show up whenever you try something new. The trick is not to silence them but to look them in the eye. Make a list of the gremlins that show up most often, and write down what tends to bring them on.

Confidence gremlins are the thoughts that quietly keep you smaller than you really are.

Let us take the gremlin that says, I am too young for this job. Write down the assumptions that come with that thought. Are you worried that your future coworkers will not take you seriously, or that you will look weak next to them? Once you have the list, test the assumptions. Talk to people who are already in that role and see whether your skills and values really do line up with theirs.

Build confidence by keeping score of your wins. Try this: at the end of each day, write down one success, no matter how small, in the notes app on your phone. The exercise will train your brain to notice that success is not only the big, flashy kind.

Every small step counts when you are building something bigger than yourself.

Now that you have worked on the long game, here are a few quick tricks to make the next meeting or presentation feel easier. Watch your words, and leave out should, could, and might. Finish each sentence before you move to the next point, and spend more time listening than talking. Be in your body by standing like you mean it, and try counting to ten or taking a few slow breaths before you go in. Practice makes permanent, so rehearse any presentation or speech out loud, even if it is just to the cat.

chapters.title: Build a network that actually works
chapters.text:
Who is more likely to get ahead, a skilled person or a well-connected one? The honest answer is that the well-connected person usually wins, and that is not a flaw in the system, it is just how the world works. Skills get you in the room, but other people open the doors. Another myth worth killing is the idea that networking means working a crowded room and making small talk. Real networking is about people helping people, and it can happen anywhere, at a social event, at your office, or even at a birthday party.

Surround yourself with people who know things you do not. They will see angles that you would miss.

There are many ways to grow a useful network. Make your interests known, because people can only help you if they know what you care about. Write down five things you are passionate about and start telling the people around you. Start a side project, and try to pick one that is nothing like your day job. A side project brings in new people, and it gives you a story to tell. Go where the people you want to meet already are. Search for them on LinkedIn, show up to talks in your area, and join the online forums where the real conversations happen. Find a sponsor, which is a person who will speak up for you when you are not in the room. Sponsors do not show up on demand, so you earn one by doing strong work on a project that matters to them. And remember that networking goes both ways. Even when your title is small, you can share what you know. If you learned a few things about copywriting on a side project, turn that into a short post on a forum or a blog.

People with sponsors tend to feel braver when they ask for a raise or a new kind of role.

chapters.title: Picture the future you want
chapters.text:
The last piece of the puzzle is future possibilities, and they are not the same thing as a five-year plan. In a world where entire industries change in a year, the old question, Where do you see yourself in five years, is starting to feel outdated.

The classic recipe for getting ahead, hard work plus loyalty equals promotion, is no longer enough.

Future possibilities are about what you want to do next, but the more useful question is why. Why do you want to go in a particular direction? The answer tells you more about who you are than the goal ever will.

To get a clear picture of your why, build a vision board of the things you want to try. Use the must-haves from the values exercise and the things that light you up. A few tips to make it work. Be careful when you pick the images, and only choose pictures that mean something real to you. Change the board whenever it feels right, because your vision will grow with you. Pick a format that fits your life, paper, digital, a wall, or a folder on your phone. Share the board if you want to, but remember that it is for you first.

If a vision board does not feel like your style, try writing a personal manifesto. A good manifesto has three simple parts. It should state what you believe and what you care about. It should be the kind of thing that makes you want to get up in the morning. And it should use positive language, not a list of things you are against.

A manifesto can be a story, a paragraph, or a page. There is no wrong shape. Read it again every six months, and notice what has changed and what has not. One small trick that often works is to finish the sentence, Happiness is, and let the answer guide you for a while.

chapters.title: A short note to send you on your way
chapters.text:
Do not be afraid of the squiggly line of your career. It may feel messy, and some days it will feel too messy, but that is the shape of the modern job market. Lean into the change, take the small risks, and trust the process. The sooner you let go of the old plan, the more options you open up for yourself.

Try this. Make a short action plan for the next three months. Write down the skills you want to grow, pick one or two resources to help you, and put a date next to each step. Then start a small blog or a portfolio page so that the work you are doing has a place to live online. Find two or three people who are also learning, and set up a regular call to swap notes and keep each other honest. And say yes to the next opportunity that scares you a little. It is better to look back and say, I really did that, than to wish you had.
